content:
5 min read
3 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The small habits that quietly make a huge difference once your projects grow beyond a few files.

A few years ago, I thought I was becoming a better frontend developer because I could build features faster.
Then one Friday evening, a tiny UI change took nearly four hours.
Not because it was difficult.
Because I couldn’t remember why I had written the code that way.
The component had duplicated logic, inconsistent naming, random CSS overrides, and a handful of “temporary” fixes that somehow survived months of development.
I wasn’t fighting the feature.
I was fighting my past self.
That was the day I realized something important:
Great frontend developers don’t save time by typing faster. They save time by building habits that prevent future problems.
Most of these habits don’t feel important when you’re building your first version.
But after enough bug fixes, production releases, and code reviews, you start noticing the same patterns.
These are the frontend habits that have probably saved me hundreds of hours.

1. I Stopped Writing Code for “Right Now”
Early in my career, my mindset was simple:
“If it works, ship it.”
That sounds productive.
Until three weeks later when you need to change it.
Here’s the kind of code I used to write.
❌ Bad
if (user.role === "admin") {
...
} else if (user.role === "manager") {
...
} else if (user.role === "editor") {
...
}A month later…
Someone asks you to add five more roles.
Now you’re editing multiple files and hoping you didn’t miss one.
✅ Better
const permissions = {
admin: [...],
manager: [...],
editor: [...]
};const currentPermissions = permissions[user.role];
The difference isn’t performance.
It’s maintenance.
Takeaway: Write code assuming someone — including future you — will need to change it.

2. I Started Naming Things Like Someone Else Would Read Them
For years I wrote variables like this:
const d = data;
const x = result;
const temp = [];They made sense…
For about ten minutes.
Six months later, every file felt like solving a puzzle.
Now I prefer names that explain intent.
❌ Bad
const x = users.filter(...)✅ Better
const activeUsers = users.filter(...)Reading code should feel like reading sentences.
Not decoding secrets.
Takeaway: Clear names reduce bugs before they even exist.

3. I Stopped Copy-Pasting Components
Every developer has done this.
You build a nice card component.
Need another one?
Copy.
Paste.
Change a few lines.
Repeat twenty times.
Everything feels fine…
Until the design changes.
Now you’re updating twenty files instead of one.
Instead of duplication, I started asking:
“Can this become a reusable component?”
Not every component should be reusable.
But repeated UI almost always should.
Takeaway: Every copy-paste creates future maintenance.

4. I Learned to Trust CSS More Than JavaScript
One of my biggest frontend mistakes was solving every UI problem with JavaScript.
Need spacing?
JavaScript.
Need responsive layouts?
JavaScript.
Need hover effects?
JavaScript.
Eventually I realized CSS had evolved while I wasn’t paying attention.
Things like:

• Grid

• Flexbox

• clamp()

• aspect-ratio

• Container queries

• :has()
have replaced entire chunks of JavaScript I used to write.
❌ Bad
window.addEventListener("resize", updateLayout);✅ Better
.card {
width: clamp(280px, 40vw, 500px);
}Less JavaScript.
Fewer bugs.
Better performance.
Takeaway: Every line of JavaScript you don’t write is one less line you’ll debug.

5. I Started Fixing Small Problems Immediately
I used to leave comments like this:
// TODO: Fix laterLater almost never came.
Those tiny shortcuts slowly turned into technical debt.
Now I follow a simple rule.
If something takes less than five minutes to clean up…
I do it immediately.
Rename the variable.
Remove unused code.
Delete dead files.
Simplify a function.
Small fixes compound over time.
Just like technical debt does.
Takeaway: Tiny improvements today prevent massive cleanups later.

6. I Stopped Chasing Clever Code
There was a time when I loved writing one-liners.
Something like this:
❌ Bad
const result = items.filter(x => x.active).map(x => x.name).sort().join(", ");Looks impressive.
Until you need to debug it.
Now I’d rather write code that’s boring.
Because boring code is usually readable code.
✅ Better
const activeItems = items.filter(item => item.active);
const names = activeItems.map(item => item.name);
names.sort();
const result = names.join(", ");More lines?
Yes.
More maintainable?
Absolutely.
Takeaway: Code is read far more often than it’s written.

7. I Started Reviewing My Own Pull Requests
This one changed everything.
Before creating a PR, I pretend I’m reviewing someone else’s code.
I ask myself:

• Would I understand this tomorrow?

• Is this variable name obvious?

• Can I simplify this?

• Is there duplicate logic?

• Would I approve this PR?
Surprisingly…
I catch most issues before anyone else sees them.
It also makes code reviews much faster.
Takeaway: Be your own first reviewer.

8. I Treat Console Logs Like Temporary Scaffolding
Console logging is useful.
I still use it every day.
But leaving dozens of logs in production code makes debugging harder.
❌ Bad
console.log(user);
console.log(response);
console.log("clicked");
console.log(state);Now the console becomes noise.
Instead, log intentionally.
Or better yet…
Use your browser’s debugger.
It gives you far more context than endless logs.
Takeaway: Good debugging tools beat excessive logging.

9. I Organise Files Before the Project Gets Big
Every project starts organised.
Then features arrive.
Deadlines happen.
Soon you’re searching for a component that might be inside one of five folders.
I’ve learned to organize continuously instead of waiting for “cleanup week.”
Small improvements include:

• Group related components.

• Keep filenames predictable.

• Avoid deeply nested folders.

• Delete unused files regularly.
Future navigation becomes effortless.
Takeaway: Folder structure is part of the developer experience.

10. I Optimise for Future Me
This is the biggest lesson.
Every decision creates one of two outcomes.
Either future you says:
“Nice… this is easy.”
Or…
“Why did I do this?”
Whenever I’m writing code now, I imagine opening that file six months later.
If something feels confusing today…
It’ll feel impossible later.
That tiny mental shift has improved nearly every project I’ve worked on.
Takeaway: Your future self is one of your most important teammates.

The Habit That Changed Everything
None of these habits are revolutionary.
You won’t find them in framework documentation.
Nobody talks about them at conferences as much as the newest library.
Yet these tiny decisions quietly determine whether a project stays enjoyable after six months — or becomes something everyone is afraid to touch.
Looking back, I didn’t become faster because I learned another framework.
I became faster because I made fewer mistakes that I’d have to fix later.
That’s the real productivity boost.
Not writing more code.
Writing code that lets your future self keep moving instead of cleaning up yesterday’s shortcuts.
So here’s a challenge:
The next time you open an old file, don’t ask, “Does it work?”
Ask, “Would I be happy if every file in this project looked like this?”
Your answer says more about the quality of your codebase than any benchmark ever will.
